title: AI Agents, Tools, and Automation Concepts

learning_objectives: Understand what an AI agent is and how workflows use tools and decisions.

blocks.title: Week 9 Focus
blocks.content: Students separate trigger, inputs, tools, decisions, outputs, and review points.

items.label: Agent vs Chatbot
items.description: Understand how multi-step behavior differs from simple chat response.

items.label: Workflow Breakdown
items.description: Identify trigger, tools, steps, and outputs.

items.label: Human Oversight
items.description: Preserve review and accountability in automation.

items.label: Trigger
items.description: The event that starts the workflow.

items.label: Tool
items.description: A system or service the workflow uses.

items.label: Decision
items.description: A branch that changes what happens next.

items.label: Review
items.description: A human checkpoint before the final result is used.

blocks.title: Practical Framing
blocks.content: Students do not need to code an advanced agent. They need to design a credible beginner workflow.

activity.title: Agent Workflow Design Board
activity.instructions: Design a beginner-friendly assistant workflow for a real use case.
activity.success_criteria: Include at least one decision point and one human review point.

questions.prompt: What makes an AI agent different from a simple chatbot?
options: It only answers in one sentence
It can handle multi-step tasks with tools or decisions
questions.explanation: Agent-like systems usually coordinate multi-step work, tools, or decisions.

questions.prompt: Which item is an important part of an agentic workflow breakdown?
questions.explanation: Trigger is a key part of understanding when and how a workflow begins.

questions.prompt: Why should an agent workflow still include human review?
options: Because humans are part of hardware
Because automation can still make mistakes or unsafe decisions
Because review removes all AI value
questions.explanation: Human review is still important for safety, accuracy, and accountability.

reflection.prompt: Describe a simple assistant workflow for your field. Include trigger, tool use, output, and one review point.
reflection.placeholder: A student FAQ assistant starts when a question is submitted, checks a knowledge source, drafts an answer, and a staff member reviews it.

teacher_notes: Keep the examples practical such as student FAQ workflows or appointment helpers.
Push students to identify review points, not just automated steps.
